3A_Bibliographic.citation: Zhao, Y., Paulus, M. P., Bhatt, S., & Potenza, M. N. (2024). Screen time, sleep, brain structural neurobiology, and sequential associations with child and adolescent psychopathology: Insights from the ABCD study. Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 13(3).

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed longitudinal data from baseline to Year 2 follow-up (data were collected from September 10, 2018, to December 31, 2019, involving participants aged 9-12 years) of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (5.0 release). The final sample was then divided into discovery (n = 2,359, 1,054 girls) and replication (n = 2,282, 997 girls) sets.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Total n=4,641 split into discovery and replication samples.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen media activity (SMA) - total hours per typical day
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV shows/movies, videos, video games, texting, social networking sites, video chatting
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (average of typical weekday and weekend total screen times)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The SMA measure consisted of youth self-reports of the total number of hours spent on non-school-related SMA on a typical day, calculated as average of a typical weekday and weekend total screen times. SMA mainly included time spent watching TV shows/movies, watching videos, playing video games, texting on electronic devices, visiting social networking sites, and video-chatting.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Internalizing and externalizing problems (CBCL), sleep duration, DIMS
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) internalizing and externalizing T-scores; Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (SDSC) - sleep duration and DIMS subscale
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) was used to assess internalizing and externalizing problems. Internalizing problems refer to inwardly directed concerns such as anxiety, depression, and somatic disturbances, while externalizing problems encompass outwardly directed behaviors like rule-breaking and aggressive conduct.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcome domains: mental health (internalizing/externalizing) and sleep. Brain covariation also examined as mediator.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) with DWLS estimator in lavaan; mediation analysis for brain-externalizing-SMA pathway
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: race/ethnicity, sex, family income, age, physical pubertal development stage
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We employed separate Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs). These models enabled us to examine the reciprocal predictive relationships between psychopathology domains, sleep difficulties (specifically, DIMS and sleep duration), and SMA. Structural equation modeling was conducted using the R lavaan package v0.6. We used a diagonally weighted least squares estimator. Significance was evaluated using two-sided hypothesis-testing at an alpha of 0.05.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Discovery and replication sample design used.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: higher SMA at 9-10 years predicted shorter sleep duration at age 10-11 years (discovery: beta = -0.10 [95%CI: -0.16, -0.03], p = 0.004)
a shorter sleep duration at 9-10 years significantly predicted higher SMA at 10-11 years (discovery: beta = -0.11 [95%CI: -0.18, -0.04], p = 0.001)
Greater externalizing symptoms at 10-11 years predicted higher DIMS (discovery: beta = 0.11 [95%CI: 0.04, 0.19], p = 0.002)
more SMA (discovery: beta = 0.07 [95%CI: 0.01, 0.13], p = 0.014) at 11-12 years

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Discovery sample; concurrent CLPM with internalizing and externalizing
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: both SMA (discovery: beta = 0.05 [95%CI: 0.02, 0.09], p = 0.003) at 9-10 years predicted externalizing problems at 10-11 years
Externalizing symptoms at 10-11 years robustly predicted internalizing symptoms one year later (discovery: beta = 0.09 [95%CI: 0.05, 0.13], p < 0.001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: SMA at year 2 via externalizing (mediation indirect effect)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Indirect effect in mediation model (discovery sample)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a significant mediating role of externalizing behavior (discovery: indirect effect = 0.032, [95%CI: 0.003, 0.067], p-value = 0.030)

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Effect sizes are small (beta 0.05-0.11) but magnitude is not discussed or contextualized. Discovery/replication approach is a strength.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: The findings emphasize the need for understanding individual differences and developing and implementing integrated strategies addressing both sleep concerns and screen time to mitigate potential impacts on psychopathology.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: The findings emphasize the need for understanding individual differences and developing and implementing integrated strategies addressing both sleep concerns and screen time to mitigate potential impacts on psychopathology.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on the bidirectional relationship between SMA and sleep duration in late childhood, and the pivotal role of externalizing symptoms as a predictor of both future SMA and internalizing problems. The paper frames SMA as both a consequence and contributor within a complex developmental system, with brain structural covariation as a distal antecedent. Clinical implications emphasize early identification and integrated intervention strategies addressing both sleep and screen time. Effect sizes (beta 0.05-0.11) are not explicitly contextualized as small.
